content.text: Consuming a REST API means writing client code that constructs HTTP requests, sends them to a server, handles responses, and manages the concerns that arise in production: authentication, retries, rate limiting, error handling, and connection efficiency. Getting these details right is the difference between a brittle integration that breaks under load and a robust client that survives network failures gracefully.
A REST API client is any code that sends HTTP requests to a REST server and processes the responses. In Python, this means using

as the HTTP transport layer, wrapping calls in a service class that handles serialization and error mapping, and wiring in cross-cutting concerns (authentication, retry, logging) at the client or adapter layer.
REST consumption patterns exist at several levels of abstraction. At the lowest level, raw

calls are sufficient for scripts. At the highest level, generated API clients (from OpenAPI specs) provide typed methods and automatically handle authentication. Most production applications live in between: a hand-written service class wrapping

Every agentic AI system that calls external tools calls REST APIs. An agent that searches the web, reads a calendar, sends an email, or queries a database is almost certainly calling REST endpoints. How those calls are structured — whether they retry on transient errors, honour rate limits, handle malformed responses, and authenticate correctly — determines whether the agent works reliably in production.

Beyond agents, any backend service that integrates with third-party providers (payment processors, messaging platforms, analytics services, LLM APIs) is a REST API consumer. The patterns for consuming APIs robustly are the same regardless of the provider.
Think of an HTTP client session as a

. Rather than addressing and buying a stamp for every letter individually (creating a new connection for every request), a session reuses the established address and prepaid stamp arrangement (TCP connection, auth headers, base URL). You drop letters in (requests) and receive replies (responses) efficiently.
The service layer around the HTTP client is a
: it converts domain concepts ("get the user with ID 42") into HTTP specifics (
with the right headers), and converts HTTP responses (status code 200, JSON body) back into domain objects or exceptions.
— wrapping API calls in a service class keeps HTTP details out of business logic, making both easier to test and change.
— HTTP sessions maintain a connection pool, dramatically reducing latency for repeated calls compared to opening a new connection each time.
— authentication headers, retry logic, and request logging can be configured once on the client rather than scattered across every call site.
— a service class wrapping an HTTP client can be tested with a mock transport, enabling unit testing without real API calls.
— parsing JSON responses into Pydantic models gives typed access to response fields and catches schema mismatches at the boundary.
— wrapping an API in a service class takes time. For a one-off script, inline
— if the API schema changes, the client code and any Pydantic models must be updated.
— auto-generated OpenAPI clients handle common cases but can be hard to extend for non-standard authentication or retry behaviour.
— too few connections causes queuing; too many overwhelms the server or triggers rate limiting.
httpx is the recommended HTTP client for Python. It supports both synchronous and async usage, has a similar interface to
, and provides first-class HTTP/2 support. Use
for async code. Always use a context manager to ensure the connection pool is closed cleanly.
The most common REST authentication patterns are Bearer tokens (JWT or opaque), API keys in headers or query parameters, and HTTP Basic authentication.
on the client constructor, so credentials are sent automatically with every request.
Raise exceptions on non-2xx status codes with
. Map responses to Pydantic models for type safety. Catch
at the service layer and convert them to domain exceptions.

attrs.alt: REST API client architecture: application, HTTP library, and API

content.text: The application layer calls a service class using domain concepts. The service class translates to HTTP and calls the library. The library manages the connection pool, TLS, and low-level transport. The response travels back through the same layers, with JSON deserialized into domain objects by the service class.
Any integration with a third-party REST API: payment processing, email, calendar, geolocation, LLM providers.
Microservice-to-microservice communication where gRPC is not in use.
Calling LLM provider APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) which expose REST interfaces.
Webhooks delivery verification (calling back to a configured URL).

def iter_all_users(client: httpx.Client, base_url: str) -> Iterator[dict]:
    """Yield all users across all pages."""
    page = 1
    while True:
        response = client.get(f"{base_url}/users", params={"page": page, "per_page": 100})
        response.raise_for_status()
        data = response.json()
        users = data["items"]
        if not users:
            break
        yield from users
        if not data.get("has_next_page"):
            break
        page += 1
Calling an LLM provider REST API

An agentic research pipeline at an analytics firm consumed five REST APIs: a news aggregator, a company financials provider, a geocoding service, a currency exchange service, and an internal document store. Each API was wrapped in its own service class with a consistent interface: typed methods, domain exceptions, and automatic retry for transient errors.

The agents composed these services through dependency injection, making each step independently testable with mock service implementations. When the news aggregator changed its response schema, only the news service adapter needed updating — no agent code changed. The pipeline processed 50,000 requests per day across the five APIs with a 99.7% success rate, with retries handling the remainder.
Creating a new client per request

Opening a new TCP connection for every HTTP request wastes 50–300ms per call in connection establishment overhead. Use a session/client object that maintains a connection pool across multiple requests.
headers. Ignoring these and retrying immediately causes cascading 429 errors and potential IP bans. Read the
header and sleep for the specified duration.
around API calls hides bugs and makes debugging impossible. Always log the error and either re-raise or convert to a domain exception with context.

try: user = svc.get_user(id)
except: user = None
Catch specific exceptions, log with context, raise domain exception
on the client. Default is no timeout; a hanging API call will block a thread/coroutine indefinitely.
instances across requests to amortize connection establishment cost.
— parse API responses into typed models immediately. Validation errors surface schema mismatches before they cause AttributeErrors deep in the call stack.
and retry only on transient errors (429, 5xx, connection errors). Never retry on 4xx (except 429).
— log the URL, method, status code, and response time for every API call to enable debugging and performance monitoring.
— if the API supports version headers (e.g.,
), pin it to prevent silent breaking changes during provider upgrades.
— never hardcode API keys. Load from environment variables or a secrets manager at runtime.
Connection pooling is the primary performance lever when consuming REST APIs.
maintains a pool of persistent connections. The pool size is configured via
with asyncio to make many API calls concurrently without blocking threads.

Rate limit handling must be proactive, not reactive. If the API enforces 100 requests/second, use a semaphore or token bucket to throttle outbound requests rather than relying on the API to reject excess requests.
The recommended Python HTTP client. Supports sync and async, HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2, connection pooling, and has an excellent test transport (
) for unit testing without real HTTP.
The original Python HTTP library. Mature, widely documented, sync-only. Use
A retry library with support for exponential backoff, conditional retries, and jitter. The standard tool for adding retry logic to REST API calls.
Generates a typed Python client from an OpenAPI specification. Useful when the API provides a spec and the team wants to skip manual client writing.

Never log request bodies that may contain PII or credentials. Log only metadata: URL, method, status code, duration. If request bodies must be logged for debugging, redact sensitive fields before logging.
Store API keys in environment variables or a secrets manager. Rotate keys on a schedule. Implement short-lived tokens (JWTs with expiry) rather than long-lived API keys where the API supports it.
Validate TLS certificates — never disable certificate verification in production. Pin certificates for high-security integrations (financial APIs, health data).
Use httpx.Client (sync) or httpx.AsyncClient (async) as the transport layer; always reuse the client across requests to benefit from connection pooling.
Wrap API calls in a service class that handles serialization, error mapping, and authentication centrally.
Validate JSON responses with Pydantic models to catch schema mismatches at the boundary rather than deep in business logic.
Retry transient errors (429, 5xx, connection errors) with exponential backoff using tenacity; never retry on 4xx errors.
Set explicit timeouts on every client to prevent indefinite blocking on slow or unresponsive APIs.
Honour rate limit headers — read Retry-After and X-RateLimit-Remaining to avoid cascading 429 responses.
Store API credentials in environment variables; log request metadata but never log request bodies that may contain secrets or PII.
